explanations.4: This references a Docker Compose file located one directory level up from the current folder using '../' to go back.
explanations.7: This specifies which user account inside the Docker container will run the development environment—in this case, a user named 'python'.
explanations.53: This tells the editor to break long lines at a specific column width (80 characters) rather than letting them extend infinitely across the screen.
explanations.67: The 'always' value means VS Code will automatically fix and organize your code every time you save, without asking you first.
explanations.85: This enables spell-checking in three languages: English, French, and Dutch, so the spell checker accepts words from all three.
explanations.89: Unlike line 67, 'explicit' means code fixes only run when you manually request them, not automatically on save.
explanations.91: Setting this to null disables VS Code's built-in code folding and allows extensions to provide their own folding logic instead.
explanations.102: These two numbers (120 and 70) create vertical guide lines in the editor to help you see when your lines are getting too long.
explanations.116: This automatically detects what programming language you're working in and applies that language's rules to new files.
explanations.124: This controls which heading levels appear in the table of contents—in this case, headings from level 2 to level 6 will be included.
explanations.139: Strict type checking means Python's type-checking tool (mypy) will be very thorough and catch more potential type-related errors.
explanations.146: These settings tell the import-sorting tool to use the 'black' profile, which matches how the Black formatter expects imports to be organized.
